planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in very well-drained, sandy, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; hesperaloe is extremely drought tolerant once established and grows naturally in the dry limestone hills of south Texas and northeastern Mexico. Never plant in wet or clay soil.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; despite its common name 'red yucca,' hesperaloe is not a true yucca but a close relative in the Asparagaceae family native to south Texas and northeastern Mexico. It forms striking rosettes of long, narrow, grass-like leaves with thread-like fibers along the margins and produces tall, arching flower stalks 4–6 feet high covered in coral-red tubular flowers from spring through fall — an outstanding hummingbird plant that blooms continuously for months. It is extraordinarily drought tolerant, heat tolerant, deer resistant, and adaptable, thriving in pure gravel or caliche where most plants fail. The yellow form ('Yellow') is an attractive alternative. One of the best xeriscape plants for the Gulf South, equally at home in formal landscapes and naturalistic plantings. Almost no maintenance required after establishment.

common_name: Rock Rose (Texas Pavonia, Rose Mallow Pavonia)

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; pavonia is native to the Edwards Plateau and south Texas and is adapted to alkaline limestone soils — it actually grows better in lean, rocky conditions than in rich garden soil.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Texas pavonia is one of the most outstanding native shrubs for the Gulf Coast garden — a compact, woody perennial shrub that produces a profusion of small (1.5 inch) single rose-pink flowers with yellow stamens continuously from spring through frost, with peak bloom in summer. Each individual flower lasts only a day, but the plant produces new flowers constantly for 6+ months, creating a long-lasting display. It tolerates alkaline soil, drought, and heat and actually blooms better under mild stress than in rich, moist conditions. Deer resistant and attractive to hummingbirds and butterflies. In zone 7–8, top growth may be killed by hard freezes but roots resprout vigorously in spring. Light pruning in late winter promotes compact, bushy growth.

common_name: Gulf Coast Muhly (Gulf Muhly, Gulf Hairgrass)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container transplants in spring in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Coast muhly grows naturally in coastal flatwoods, pine savannas, and well-drained sandy areas from the Carolinas to Texas and tolerates both drought and brief flooding.

directions: Plant in well-drained, sandy, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; Gulf Coast muhly is one of the most spectacular native grasses of the southeastern coastal plain, producing dense tufts of fine, arching leaves topped with extraordinary clouds of pink-purple to reddish plumes in October–November — the fall bloom is breathtaking in mass plantings, creating a haze of color resembling smoke drifting over the landscape at sunset. It is the native counterpart to the widely planted inland pink muhly (M. capillaris) but is even better adapted to the sandy, humid Gulf Coast environment and more salt-tolerant. The fine-textured foliage is attractive throughout the growing season. Shear to 4–6 inches in late winter before new growth begins. An outstanding fall-blooming native for the Gulf Coast landscape.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; Lindheimer's muhly is native to limestone-based soils of the Edwards Plateau and south-central Texas and is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant once established.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Lindheimer's muhly is one of the most striking large ornamental grasses native to Texas, forming graceful, arching clumps of narrow blue-green leaves 3–4 feet tall that turn golden-tan in fall, topped with silky silver-gray flower plumes 4–5 feet high from fall through winter. Named for Ferdinand Lindheimer, the pioneering Texas botanist, it is remarkably tough and adaptable — thriving in heat, drought, alkaline soils, and even rocky caliche where most ornamental grasses fail. The large, dramatic clumps provide outstanding winter structure and movement. It is somewhat tolerant of clay soils if drainage is adequate. Cut to 6 inches in late winter before new growth begins; established clumps need division only every 4–5 years when they become hollow in the center.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; forsythia sage is a large plant that needs room — it can grow 6–8 feet tall in a single season. In zone 8a, cut to the ground in winter and mulch; roots are reliably hardy to at least 15°F.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; forsythia sage is a bold, large-growing Mexican salvia that produces masses of bright clear-yellow flowers in dense whorls up tall, square stems from October through frost — its bright yellow blooms at the same time forsythia blooms in spring create a striking parallel, giving rise to its common name. The plant is remarkable for its tremendous size — growing 5–8 feet tall in a single season — and for its late-season bloom when most other plants are finishing. The large, heart-shaped leaves are powerfully aromatic when bruised. It is an outstanding hummingbird plant in fall when migrating birds need energy. The stems die back after frost but resprout vigorously from established root crowns in spring. One of the finest large fall-blooming perennials for the Gulf South.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container transplants in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in partial shade; cedar sage is one of the few native salvias that genuinely prefers shade and is found growing naturally under cedar (Juniperus) and oak woodlands in central Texas.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, dry to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; cedar sage is a compact, low-growing native perennial of the limestone woodlands of the Texas Hill Country and Edwards Plateau — one of the very few native salvias that grows naturally in shade under Ashe juniper (cedar) and live oak canopies, making it exceptionally valuable for shaded Texas gardens. The small, rounded, hairy leaves are fragrant, and the brilliant red tubular flowers are produced on short spikes from spring through fall in shade conditions where few other flowering plants thrive. It is a critical hummingbird food plant. The plant stays compact and low (12–18 inches), spreads slowly by underground stolons, and requires almost no maintenance in well-drained shaded sites. Combine with turk's cap and inland sea oats for a beautiful dry shade planting.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in fall in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, dry to moderately dry soil in full sun to partial shade; agarita grows naturally on rocky limestone hillsides and is extremely drought tolerant once established. Transplanting is best done in fall or early spring — avoid disturbing roots unnecessarily.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, dry soil in full sun to partial shade; agarita is a classic native Texas evergreen shrub with small, three-lobed, holly-like leaves (each with sharp spines) arranged in a distinctive trifoliate pattern — it looks like a small holly with fierce spines and makes an impenetrable wildlife barrier hedge. In spring it produces clusters of small, intensely fragrant yellow flowers, followed by brilliant red tart berries in late spring that are relished by birds and made by Texas foragers into highly prized jelly. The flavor is tart-sweet, similar to a currant. The berries ripen and fall quickly — traditionally harvested by spreading a tarp under the shrub and shaking the branches. Deer browse it rarely due to the spines. A tough, beautiful, and ecologically valuable native for difficult dry spots in zone 6–9.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in well-drained to xeric, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; sotol requires excellent drainage and grows best in rocky, limestone-based soils. It is extremely slow-growing and patience is required — established plants can live for many decades.

directions: Plant in well-drained, rocky, lean, alkaline soil in full sun; Texas sotol is a dramatic native xeriscape plant of the Chihuahuan Desert and Edwards Plateau, forming a globe of many hundreds of narrow, finely toothed, spoon-based leaves with serrated margins and a distinctive dried-flower-arrangement appearance year-round. Established plants occasionally send up towering flower stalks 10–15 feet tall bearing dense clusters of small white flowers in summer, then produce fruit and seeds over the following months before dying back — the vegetative rosette survives and eventually produces another stalk. The base of the leaves was a critical food staple for the Chihuahuan people who roasted and ate the heart (similar to agave); the leaves are used to make sotol fiber and baskets. Slow-growing but extremely long-lived and virtually maintenance-free once established.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings easily in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Barbados gooseberry is a primitive (leafy) cactus that grows as a woody climbing vine and can be trained on a fence or trellis. It is frost tender and suited to zone 9b–12 in the ground, or grown as a container plant.

directions: Root cuttings in sandy, well-drained soil in full sun; Barbados gooseberry is one of the most botanically fascinating of all edible plants — a primitive cactus (family Cactaceae) that retains true persistent leaves unlike all other cacti, making it a living link between leafy plants and the succulent cacti. The woody, thorny scrambling vine can reach 30 feet when climbing, with oval, slightly succulent leaves and masses of fragrant white to pale pink flowers in fall. The yellow to orange fruits ripen to 0.75 inches with multiple seeds embedded in pulp with a pleasant acid-sweet, citrus-like flavor — used fresh, in jams, and as a vegetable cooked with rice in the Caribbean. The young leaves are edible as a potherb. In South America the leaves are consumed as 'ora-pro-nobis,' valued for their exceptionally high protein content (25% dry weight). An unusual and productive edible vine for frost-free gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to alkaline, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bamboo muhly is native to Arizona, New Mexico, and Mexico and tolerates both drought and light shade, making it versatile for difficult spots.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean soil in full sun to partial shade; bamboo muhly is a large, feathery ornamental grass that is often mistaken for young bamboo at first glance — the numerous, fine, arching stems bear tiny delicate leaves and produce an airy, light-filtering visual effect remarkably similar to a small bamboo clump but without the invasive spreading habit. It grows naturally in rocky canyon bottoms and streamside slopes in the Sonoran and Chihuahuan Desert region, where it tolerates both drought and periodic flooding. Unlike most ornamental grasses that are grown primarily for fall plumes, bamboo muhly is grown mainly for its outstanding texture and motion — a billowing cloud of delicate stems that moves with the slightest breeze. It tolerates light shade and makes a superb screen or specimen. Cut back to 12 inches in late winter.
